question: Define cache efficiency and explain the metrics used to measure it.
expectedPoints: Cache efficiency is the effectiveness of cache in reducing memory access time
Measured using hit rate (hits/total accesses) or miss rate (misses/total accesses)
AMAT = Hit time + (Miss rate × Miss penalty) is the key metric
Higher hit rate = better efficiency

question: Explain the three types of cache misses with examples.
expectedPoints: Compulsory/Cold miss: First access to data never in cache
Capacity miss: Working set larger than cache size
Conflict miss: Multiple blocks mapping to same cache line in set-associative or direct-mapped cache
Explain how each can be reduced

question: Differentiate between temporal and spatial locality with examples.
expectedPoints: Temporal locality: Recently accessed items likely accessed again (loop variables)
Spatial locality: Items near recently accessed location likely accessed soon (array traversal)
Explain how cache exploits both
Examples from program code

question: Compare write-through and write-back cache policies.
expectedPoints: Write-through: Updates both cache and main memory simultaneously
Write-back: Updates cache first, main memory updated on eviction
Write-back is faster but needs dirty bit
Write-through is simpler, ensures consistency
Trade-offs in performance and complexity

question: Explain LRU cache replacement policy and its advantages.
expectedPoints: LRU evicts least recently used block
Advantage: Exploits temporal locality effectively
Hardware implementation complexity for high associativity
Trade-off between accuracy and hardware cost

question: How is cache efficiency measured in microcontroller systems?
expectedPoints: Using performance counters in hardware
Benchmark programs to measure execution time
Hit/miss ratio measurement
Power consumption analysis
Real-time response metrics for embedded systems

question: What is the importance of cache coherency in multi-core microcontrollers?
expectedPoints: Multiple cores may have private caches
Cache coherency ensures consistent view of memory
Write by one core must be visible to others
Invalidation or update protocols needed
Important for shared data structures

question: Explain the formula for Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) and discuss how cache parameters affect it. A system has L1 cache with hit time of 1 cycle, miss rate of 5%, and miss penalty of 100 cycles. Calculate the AMAT.
expectedPoints: AMAT = Hit time + (Miss rate × Miss penalty) formula explanation
Hit time: time to access cache on hit
Miss rate: probability of cache miss
Miss penalty: extra time on miss to fetch from memory
Calculation: AMAT = 1 + (0.05 × 100) = 6 cycles
Compare to no cache: 100 cycles
Speedup = 100/6 ≈ 16.7x
Discuss how each parameter affects AMAT

question: Compare direct-mapped, set-associative, and fully-associative cache mapping techniques with respect to hardware complexity, hit rate, and access time.
expectedPoints: Direct-mapped: simple hardware (1 way), fastest access, highest miss rate due to conflicts
Set-associative: balanced (n ways), moderate complexity, better hit rate than direct-mapped
Fully-associative: best hit rate, most complex hardware (compares all tags)
Trade-off triangle: speed vs hit rate vs hardware cost
Examples and practical applications in microcontrollers

question: Discuss various cache optimization techniques used in microcontroller systems to improve efficiency.
expectedPoints: Increasing block size (spatial locality)
Increasing associativity (reduces conflicts)
Multi-level cache hierarchy (L1, L2)
Write buffer to reduce write stalls
Victim cache for displaced blocks
Hardware/software prefetching
Cache bypassing for streaming data
Techniques appropriate for embedded/microcontroller constraints

question: A microcontroller system has the following specifications: L1 cache: 4KB, direct-mapped, 64-byte blocks, L2 cache: 64KB, 4-way set-associative. For a program accessing array elements sequentially, analyze the cache behavior and estimate efficiency.
expectedPoints: L1: 4KB/64B = 64 lines, index = 6 bits, offset = 6 bits
Sequential access exploits spatial locality
Block prefetching helps subsequent accesses
L2 has more lines and associativity, reduces conflict misses
Calculate hit rate estimation for sequential pattern
Compare L1 vs L2 contribution to performance
Real-world implications for embedded code optimization
